text: Total laparoscopic excision of type I choledochal cyst with row and y hepatic odigenostomy from University of Florida College of Medicine in Jacksonville. Thirty-eight-year-old female patient presented with repeated episodes of upper abdominal pain. She was morbidly obese with multiple comorbidities. ERCP was done and confirmed the diagnosis of choledochal cyst. The MRCP also was done and again the same diagnosis, type I choledochal cyst. You can see outpouching of the upper part of the cyst which can make some challenge during the dissection and the lower common bile duct intrapancreatic part as well. Per distribution surgeon on the right side, assistance on the left side as you see in the picture.

The surgeon performs diagnostic laparoscopy and dissects the choledochal cyst from surrounding structures using hooked diathermy. Laparoscopic ultrasound is utilized to delineate the anatomy, revealing thickened cyst walls and surrounding blood vessels, including the hepatic artery and portal vein. The cystic duct is isolated and divided, and dissection continues on the medial part of the cyst from the portohepatic vessels using hook diathermy and a Maryland dissector. The anterior wall of the portal vein is exposed during lateral dissection, with meticulous technique due to chronic inflammation and scarring.

The surgeon dissects the inferior part of the choledochal cyst from the duodenum and the head of the pancreas using a Maryland dissector and hook diathermy. A Penrose drain is used for handling, and the intrapancreatic part of the common bile duct is carefully dissected. The thinnest part of the intrapancreatic common bile duct is stapled using an endo-GIA stapler. After stapling, the choledochal cyst and its posterior attachments are further dissected using the Maryland dissector and hook diathermy. The medial aspect of the cyst and its attachment to the porta-hepatous vessels are meticulously dissected with the aid of a suction tip for counter-traction. The out-pouching of the choledochal cyst at its cephalad part is visible.

The surgeon opens the choledochal cyst to identify the opening of the common hepatic duct. The cyst wall is separated from the common hepatic duct, and the specimen is removed through an endobag. The surgeon inspects the common hepatic duct opening to ensure the confluence is intact.

The surgeon creates a Roux limb by transecting the jejunum 50 centimeters distal to the duodenal-jejunal flexure using a GIS stapler. The mesentery is divided perpendicularly to ensure the Roux limb can reach the right upper quadrant without tension. A jejuno-jejunostomy is performed between the biliopancreatic limb and the Roux limb, with the common enterotomy approximated and stapled. An anti-obstruction stitch is placed, and the mesentery is closed in a running fashion.

The surgeon divides the transverse mesocolon to create a defect to the left of the middle colic vessels, allowing the end of the roux limb to be pulled through towards the right upper quadrant. An enterotomy is created on the roux limb, and a hepatico-jejunostomy is performed using multiple interrupted absorbable sutures, starting with the angles, followed by the posterior and anterior walls. An anchoring stitch is placed between the periductal tissue and the anti-mesenteric surface of the roux limb to reduce tension. Finally, the defect in the transverse mesocolon is closed around the roux limb to prevent herniation.

Colocystectomy is completed and a drain is placed in the right upper quadrant. Post-operative course, operative time was two hundred fifty minutes. The patient was out of bed same day of the procedure. She was discharged post-op day two, followed in the clinic with good recovery.
